english: ing or epidemic catarrh. — 2. Cs. = 。čám-pa (cam pa) accord, accordance.
slowly, by degrees, gradually Schr. (cf. čem-mé (cem me)).

termin. of ča (ca), 1. into parts, e.g. bgó-ba (bgo ba) to divide into parts. — 2. as an equal, as a match, ...la čar mi pód (la car mi pod) he is not an equal to, cannot come up to ... Thgy.; ...dań stóń-prag-čar mi nye (...dang stong prag car mi nye) prob.: he does not come up to ... at all (lit. not for the thousandth part) Pth.; so in a similar manner: brgyai čar yań mi sleb (brgyai car yang mi sleb) Tar. — 3. affixed to numerals, and sometimes, though less correctly, written čhar (char), q.v. The terminations of the cases may be affixed to it: lńa čár-gyis (lnga car gyis) every fifth day Thgy.

also čár-pa (car pa), 1. rain, čar čén-po (car cen po) a plentiful rain, čar drág-po (car drag po), or drag-čár (drag car) a heavy rain; čar čén-pas (car cen pas) or čé-bas (ce bas) as it rained heavily Pth.; čar 。bébs-pa (car bebs pa) to cause to rain; čar 。bab (car bab) it rains, W. *čár-pa yoń* (*car pa yong*); čár-gyi rgyun (car gyi rgyun) a sudden or violent shower of rain Tar. — 2. at Kyelang for watering-pot; this utensil having never been seen there before, the word was at first applied to it jestingly, but is now generally adopted; ču-tság (cu tsag) 'water-sieve' would be more correct. Comp. čar-skyibs (car skyibs) a shelter, pent-roof, protecting from rain. — čar-kébs (car kebs) dress against rain, rain-cloak. — čhár-čan (char can), čhár-ldan (char ldan) rainy Cs. — čhár-ču (char cu) rain-water. — čhár-dus (char dus) rainy season. — čhar-。dód (char dod) (-byeu (-byeu)) n. of a bird, water-ousel. — čhar-sprín (char sprin) a rain-threatening cloud. — *čar-bhí* (*car bhi*) (?) C. rain-cloak. — čhar-rlúń (char rlung) rain and wind Cs. — *čhar-šiń* (*char shin*) = čar-skyibs (car skyibs) W. — čhar-lén (char len) the coping or water-tile of a wall Cs.
sku-čál (sku cal) resp. belly, abdomen, Cs.

(Sch. čás-ka (cas ka)) cf. ča (ca) III., 1. thing, tool, requisite etc., sé-mo-do-la sogs-pai čás-kyis brgyán-te (se mo do la sogs pai cas kyis brgyan te) adorned with ornaments of pearls and other things Mil.; dga-stón-gyi čas rgya čén-po (dga ston gyi cas rgya cen po) grand festival arrangements; čas dé-rnams bšig (cas de rnams bshig) overturn the whole affair! Glr.; bág-mar rdzóń-bai čas (bag mar rdzong bai cas) things to be given to her as a dowry Tar. 121, 5; lčágs-čas (lcags cas) iron tools or utensils; ltó-čas (lto cas) food; dmag-čas (dmag cas) military stores, requisites for war Pth.; 。tsó-čas (tso cas) provisions Mil.; lág-čas (lag cas) tool, instrument Cs. — 2. dress, garment, pó-čas (po cas) man's dress; čas-gós (cas gos), W. *gon-če* (*gon ce*), coat, dress; in a more general sense: appearance, form, shape, búd-med-kyi čás-su byáste (bud med kyi cas su byaste) appearing in the shape of a woman Glr.; hór-čas byed (hor cas byed) he puts on a Mongol dress Ma.; bú-moi čás-su žugs (bu moi cas su zhugs) he puts on a girl's dress, disguises himself as a girl Glr., Pth.; čas sgyúr-ba (cas sgyur ba) to put on, to assume another dress.

originally the pf. of čá-ba (ca ba), but always used as a separate vb. 1. to set out, set forth, depart, čas dgós-par (cas dgos par) as I must depart from here Thgy.; bód-du čás-so (bod du cas so) they set out for Tibet Glr.; dus-gčíg-tu čás-so (dus gcig tu cas so) they departed at the same time Dzl.; čás-su 。júg-pa (cas su jug pa) to send away, dispatch; mgyógs-čas gtóń-ba (mgyogs cas gtong ba) to rush, run towards. — 2. to set about, to begin, 。gsód-par (gsod par) to kill; 。gró-bar čás-pa-las (gro bar cas pa las) when he made arrangements to depart Dzl.; also in the following manner; da pyír 。dóń-ńo žes čás-pa (da pyir dong ngo zhes cas pa) 'now we will return' they said, making preparations, or: saying thus, they made preparations Dzl.; tugs čás-so (tugs cas so) he had set his mind on departing Mil.

onomatopoetic word for snuffing up scents by the nose; *žim-žím dri-ma či-li-li kyer* (*zhim zhim dri ma ci li li kyer*) C. sweet odours of cakes are meeting us; mé-tog dri-ma či-li-li (me tog dri ma ci li li) the perfumes of flowers are perceptible Mil.
